collisions.paper: Chanin et al., 2024. A is for Absorption: Studying Feature Splitting and Absorption in Sparse Autoencoders. arXiv:2409.14507 (NeurIPS 2024)
collisions.overlap: Foundational absorption measurement; descriptive width/L0 dependence (Figs 7b/9b/9c) but NO formal confound control, NO partial correlations, NO mediation analysis

collisions.paper: Karvonen et al., 2025. SAEBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Sparse Autoencoders. arXiv:2503.09532
collisions.overlap: Benchmarks absorption across 3 widths x 6 sparsities grid; observes inverse scaling but NO formal causal analysis of confounds

collisions.paper: Gao et al., 2024. Scaling and Evaluating Sparse Autoencoders. arXiv:2406.04093 (ICLR 2025)
collisions.overlap: SAE scaling laws for quality vs width and L0 but does not analyze absorption as mediator

collisions.paper: Chanin et al., 2025. Feature Hedging: Correlated Features Break Narrow Sparse Autoencoders. arXiv:2505.11756
collisions.overlap: Identifies width-absorption interaction via hedging mechanism; shows narrow SAEs suffer hedging not absorption

collisions.paper: Bussmann et al., 2025. Learning Multi-Level Features with Matryoshka Sparse Autoencoders. arXiv:2503.17547 (ICML 2025)
collisions.overlap: Architecture that reduces absorption; evaluates on SAEBench first-letter metric

collisions.paper: Vig et al., 2020; Meng et al., 2022. Causal mediation analysis in mechanistic interpretability
collisions.overlap: Uses causal mediation for circuit analysis (which MODEL components mediate predictions), NOT for SAE hyperparameter confound control

candidates.differentiation_notes: The core novelty is methodological: transplanting epidemiological causal inference (mediation analysis, Rosenbaum sensitivity bounds, propensity matching, Bradford Hill criteria) to SAE evaluation. Zero prior art applies these methods to SAE quality assessment. The empirical question (does absorption causally mediate quality or is it confounded with L0?) has been raised informally but never formally tested. Existing work provides descriptive correlations; this proposal provides causal inference.

collisions.paper: Chanin et al., 2024. A is for Absorption. arXiv:2409.14507 (NeurIPS 2024)
collisions.overlap: Figures 9b/9c show absorption vs L0 at different widths -- descriptive 2D scatter plots, NOT formal surface fitting or interaction testing

collisions.paper: Karvonen et al., 2025. SAEBench. arXiv:2503.09532
collisions.overlap: 3 widths x 6 sparsities grid exists (18 data points per architecture); no formal 2D surface analysis published

collisions.paper: Chanin et al., 2025. Feature Hedging. arXiv:2505.11756
collisions.overlap: Hedging regime explicitly named and characterized; narrow SAEs suffer hedging, wide SAEs suffer absorption

collisions.paper: 2025. Understanding SAE Scaling in the Presence of Feature Manifolds. arXiv:2509.02565
collisions.overlap: Studies SAE scaling regimes but focuses on reconstruction and capacity allocation, not absorption specifically

candidates.differentiation_notes: Novelty rests on: (a) formal GAM surface fitting rather than descriptive scatter plots, (b) interaction term test for nonlinear joint width-L0 effects, (c) gradient analysis for phase boundary detection, (d) scaling to 200+ SAEs vs SAEBench's 18 grid points. Must acknowledge Feature Hedging for the hedging regime naming.

collisions.paper: Chanin et al., 2024. A is for Absorption. arXiv:2409.14507 (NeurIPS 2024)
collisions.overlap: Absorption metric methodology (same approach, different domain); explicitly notes single-task limitation

collisions.paper: Chaudhary & Geiger, 2024. Evaluating Open-Source Sparse Autoencoders on Disentangling Factual Knowledge in GPT-2 Small. arXiv:2409.04478
collisions.overlap: Uses RAVEL + SAE evaluation on city-country features, but measures DISENTANGLEMENT (intervention-based attribute transfer), NOT absorption (features failing to fire). Fundamentally different phenomenon.

collisions.paper: Karvonen et al., 2025. SAEBench. arXiv:2503.09532
collisions.overlap: Absorption metric implementation (first-letter only); RAVEL metric (disentanglement only, not absorption)

collisions.paper: Korznikov et al., 2025. OrtSAE. arXiv:2509.22033
collisions.overlap: Tests absorption on first-letter task only despite calling for cross-domain validation

collisions.paper: Kurochkin et al., 2025. KronSAE. arXiv:2505.22255
collisions.overlap: Tests absorption on first-letter task only

candidates.differentiation_notes: Fills the most explicitly called-for gap: 5+ papers note single-task limitation of absorption measurement. Critical to distinguish from Chaudhary & Geiger (2024): they measure disentanglement (can you intervene?), this measures absorption (do features fail to fire?). Shuffled-hierarchy control and hierarchy-sharpness correlation are entirely novel.

collisions.paper: Chanin et al., 2024. A is for Absorption. arXiv:2409.14507 (NeurIPS 2024)
collisions.overlap: Feature absorption phenomenon (this proposes a theoretical framework to explain it)

collisions.paper: Chanin et al., 2025. Feature Hedging. arXiv:2505.11756
collisions.overlap: Related failure mode; hedging could be viewed as a competition outcome

collisions.paper: Barbier et al., 2023. Competitive exclusion and Hebbian couplings in random generalised Lotka-Volterra systems. arXiv:2301.11703
collisions.overlap: Lotka-Volterra competitive exclusion in random systems with Hebbian-like couplings; conceptually parallel but entirely different domain

candidates.differentiation_notes: Zero prior art connecting immunodominance/competitive exclusion theory to SAE feature dynamics. Most genuinely novel candidate. Risk is decorative analogy vs load-bearing theory. R* rule predictions and active suppression signature provide falsifiable tests. Does not address blocking confound or cross-domain issues, so deprioritized correctly.

collisions.paper: Chanin et al., 2025. Feature Hedging. arXiv:2505.11756
collisions.overlap: Partial challenge to absorption narrative: shows some 'absorption' in narrow SAEs may actually be hedging

collisions.paper: 2025. Sparse but Wrong: Incorrect L0 Leads to Incorrect Features in Sparse Autoencoders. arXiv:2508.16560
collisions.overlap: Shows incorrect L0 causes incorrect features, suggesting SAE pathologies may be hyperparameter-driven rather than fundamental

collisions.paper: Karvonen et al., 2025. SAEBench. arXiv:2503.09532
collisions.overlap: Notes inverse scaling of absorption with width; observes dead features inflate ReLU absorption scores

candidates.differentiation_notes: The unified deflationary narrative is novel but the individual ingredients (L0 confound, single-task limitation, measurement artifacts) are emerging in scattered recent work. Only activated if H1 AND H2 both falsified. The taxonomy correction (92.3% to corrected estimate) is unique to this project's prior iterations.

search_methods_used: Cross-referencing SAEBench, Chanin et al., OrtSAE, KronSAE, Matryoshka SAE, Feature Hedging, SynthSAEBench, RAVEL, Chaudhary & Geiger literature
key_absence_of_evidence: Mediation analysis applied to SAE evaluation hyperparameters: ZERO results
Rosenbaum sensitivity bounds for ML interpretability: ZERO results
Bradford Hill criteria for ML model evaluation: ZERO results
Feature absorption measured on RAVEL knowledge hierarchies: ZERO results
Feature absorption measured on ANY non-spelling natural language task: ZERO results
2D absorption phase surface with formal statistical fitting: ZERO results
Lotka-Volterra / immunodominance for SAE feature dynamics: ZERO results
Propensity score matching for SAE architecture comparison: ZERO results
field_velocity_warning: The SAE absorption field is evolving rapidly (Matryoshka ICML 2025, OrtSAE Sep 2025, KronSAE May 2025, Feature Hedging May 2025, ATM 2025, SynthSAEBench Feb 2026). While no paper replicates the proposal's core contributions, the window for novelty may narrow if the community moves to formal characterization. Speed of execution is advised.
